decisions.question: Should cross-game validation require new experiments or reuse existing data?
decisions.chosen: Reuse existing per-game data from stages 10 and 13, re-aggregated under different game subsets
decisions.rationale: The stage 10 and 13 experiments already evaluated all methods on all 20 games. Cross-game validation only requires re-partitioning the per-game metrics into training/held-out subsets. No new architecture search or evaluation runs are needed, making this stage computationally cheap and fast.

alternatives.option: Run new experiments where the architecture search only sees the training game subset
alternatives.reason_rejected: Would require 5-10 new full experiment campaigns (one per split). This would be the gold standard for transfer evaluation but is prohibitively expensive. The reanalysis approach provides a strong approximation.

alternatives.option: Use only the proxy task (CIFAR-100) rankings for transfer analysis
alternatives.reason_rejected: Proxy-to-game transfer was already evaluated in stage 7. Cross-game validation specifically tests game-to-game transfer, which requires per-game results.

decisions.question: How many random splits should be used?
decisions.rationale: Five splits provide a reasonable estimate of the average transfer correlation while keeping the analysis tractable. With 20 games split 50/50, there are C(20,10) = 184,756 possible splits. Five random samples give a stable estimate of the mean rho (standard error of the mean rho ~ 0.04 based on pilot analysis).

alternatives.reason_rejected: Leave-one-out produces 20 folds but each held-out set has only 1 game, making per-fold correlation impossible. The analysis would reduce to comparing each game's performance against the average of the other 19, which is less informative than the 50/50 split.
Too few to estimate the variability of transfer correlation across different game subsets. A lucky split could show high correlation while the true average is lower.

decisions.question: What Spearman's rho threshold indicates acceptable transfer?
decisions.chosen: rho >= 0.7 for random splits, rho >= 0.5 for leave-one-tier-out
decisions.rationale: A rho of 0.7 means the training-set rankings explain ~50% of the variance in held-out rankings (R^2 ~ 0.49). This is strong enough to be practically useful -- an architecture that ranks in the top 3 on training games will likely rank in the top 5 on held-out games. The lower threshold for leave-one-tier-out (0.5) accounts for the greater difficulty of cross-tier prediction.

alternatives.reason_rejected: Unrealistic for cross-game transfer in imperfect-information games. Different games have different structural requirements. A 0.9 threshold would likely be unachievable and would not reflect practical utility.

A rho of 0.3 explains only 9% of ranking variance. This would mean knowing an architecture's performance on training games provides almost no information about held-out performance. The architecture search would not be generalizable.
